text: Five laparoscopic ports are placed: one for the camera, one for the liver retractor, and three additional working ports.
The surgeon lifts the liver off the stomach using a retractor to expose the junction between the esophagus and stomach.
The surgeon begins the operation by carefully dividing the connective tissue overlying the junction of the esophagus and stomach, ensuring adjacent structures are not damaged.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector to perform the dissection, carefully separating tissue layers with precision.
The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector, which employs vibration to cut and seal tissue in vessels, instead of using electricity.
After completing the initial dissection, the surgeon enters the chest cavity and uses gentle blunt dissection to expose the esophagus.
The surgeon uses a sweeping motion with an ultrasonic dissector to divide loose tissues. Upon encountering a vessel, it is sealed using a coagulator, preventing bleeding.
The surgeon cools the tip of the instrument on the liver to prevent overheating and potential injury to adjacent organs during the dissection.
The surgeon divides the fat pad overlying the gastroesophageal junction, carefully raising the vagus nerve off the esophagus to prevent injury during the myotomy.
An eight-centimeter umbilical tape is introduced into the surgical field to measure the length of the myotomy, indicating the division of the sphincter muscle.
The myotomy extends at least six centimeters into the esophagus and two centimeters into the stomach, indicating the precise length of the muscle division.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to mark the stomach, creating visible cauterized lines on the gastric tissue.
After removing the umbilical tape, the surgeon inserts a dilator into the esophagus to facilitate the myotomy, ensuring precise division of the sphincter muscle.
The surgeon begins the myotomy on the anterior border of the esophagus, carefully identifying and separating the tissue plane at this level.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic dissector, with its cool white end sliding under the muscle layers of the esophagus, lifting them off the inner mucosal layer to prevent injury. The device effectively divides the muscle while limiting bleeding.
The division of the muscle layer extends well into the chest, reaching at least six centimeters proximal to the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon carefully divides all layers of the muscle at the esophageal sphincter to relieve the obstruction to food passage, ensuring complete separation of the sphincter muscle.
The surgeon extends the myotomy from the anterior border of the esophagus down into the anterior wall of the stomach, ensuring the muscle layers are divided to relieve obstruction.

The surgeon navigates the more complex muscle layers of the stomach, including longitudinal, circular, and diagonal layers, which are not present on the esophagus, using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to navigate the angles of the muscle layers, facilitating precise dissection during the cardiomyotomy.
The surgeon carefully dissects the muscle layers one at a time during the cardiomyotomy, progressing slowly to avoid injury to the underlying mucosa, which is visible beneath the muscle layers.
The surgeon divides the deepest layer of muscle, the diagonal fibers, using cautery, carefully lifting them off the underlying mucosa to prevent injury.
After completing the myotomy, the surgeon inspects the mucosa for any remaining muscle fibers and carefully divides them using surgical instruments.
The surgeon places sutures in areas with potential injury or risk of bleeding to prevent postoperative complications.
A light is visible inside the esophagus, illuminating the interior for inspection.

The video endoscope is used to assess the mucosa for leaks and to trans-illuminate any residual muscle fibers that have not been cut. The endoscope helps ensure that the myotomy extends sufficiently into the stomach, with marks on the inside of the esophagus providing reliable identification.
The surgeon creates an anterior wrap around the esophagus, visually securing the structure to help prevent reflux following the cardiomytomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the lateral attachments and blood vessels, freeing the stomach to increase its mobility and reduce tension on the future wrap.
The surgeon carries the dissection up to the diaphragm, focusing on the area to the left of the esophagus, ensuring careful separation of tissues in this region.
The surgeon places sutures from the stomach, completing the anterior wrap to prevent reflux, as seen through the video endoscope.
The surgeon passes sutures through the cut edge of the muscle and secures it to the diaphragm, ensuring proper positioning and stabilization.
The surgeon begins to create a wrap to cover the exposed mucosa, providing protection in case of a delayed injury.
The surgeon marsupializes the edges of the cut muscle to prevent them from scarring back together, which would reform the sphincter and potentially cause recurrent dysphagia.
The surgeon places three separate sutures to attach the wrap to the left diaphragm, securing the wrap in position.
The surgeon places three additional sutures to attach the wrap to the right side of the diaphragm, completing the wrap and covering the esophageal mucosa.
The surgeon places and ties the final sutures, completing the wrap by bringing the stomach anterior to the esophagus and myotomy, attaching it to the diaphragm.
